invariant_definition: Part, portion, or share. In Vedānta the term becomes a controlled way of saying that a dependent reality belongs to *brahman* or Bhagavān without immediately collapsing into strict identity. It therefore sits beside *prakāra*, *śakti*, *apṛthak-siddhi*, and the many forms of *bhedābheda*. A major scriptural driver is *Bhagavad-Gītā* 15.7, *mamaivāṃśo jīvaloke jīvabhūtaḥ sanātanaḥ*: the eternal *jīva* is 'my very own *aṃśa*.' Schools then ask what sort of partness this can be. Is the self a literal part of the Lord, a mode inseparable from Him, a dependent energy, or only figuratively called a part because of similarity or subordination? Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavas distinguish *svāṃśa* manifestations of Bhagavān from *vibhinnāṃśa* selves; Bhāskara reads partness through real transformation; Advaita refuses literal partness because *brahman* is partless; Tattva-vāda (the school often labeled Dvaita) rejects it because the *jīva* is eternally distinct. 'Part' is thus never enough by itself; one must specify the metaphysics of the whole.

per_school.definition: Since duality is only *māyāmātra* and *advaita* alone is *paramārtha*, scriptural part-language cannot mark a real partition within brahman [[MK 1.17](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.17)]. When the unborn reality is known, *dvaita* is no longer found, so any *aṃśa* discourse must belong to the pedagogical and empirical register opened by beginningless *māyā*, not to brahman's own nature [[MK 1.16-18](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakaraṇa.16-18)].

Rāmānuja's proem already places *aṃśa* within the *antaryāmin* grammar: the śrutis teach cit and acit as the Lord's *śakti*, *aṃśa*, *vibhūti*, *rūpa*, and *śarīra* under a single *sāmānādhikaraṇya* [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. In this usage, *aṃśa* denotes a real but inseparable mode of Bhagavān Nārāyaṇa, governed by *śarīra-śarīrin-bhāva* rather than by any detachable mereology [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

For Madhva, the *sākṣin* that apprehends difference is itself a foundational *pramāṇa*, so the distinction between *jīva* and Īśvara is *śrutisāmarthya-susthira* and not available for collapse into identity [[Anuvy. 2.3.66](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66)]. The abheda-texts are retained only on the basis of *sādṛśya*: consciousness and bliss similar to the Lord remain in the liberated *jīva*, and scriptural non-difference-language therefore works by likeness and dependence, never by *svarūpābhidā* [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)]. In the *aṃśa-adhikaraṇa*, then, *aṃśa* is read through asymmetric dependence rather than through any essential sharing of the Lord's being [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)].

Jīva Gosvāmī coordinates the *abheda-śāstras* by holding that the aggregate of *jīvas* stands to Bhagavān, through his *svābhāvika acintya-śakti*, in the position of atomic rays to the sun [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. On that basis, *vyatireka* and *avyatireka* are jointly maintained, so *aṃśa* is to be understood through dependent radiance and *śakti*, not through crude severable parthood [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Bhagavān is consequently the *parama-svarūpa* of all, as the sun is of its rays, which fixes the devotional completion of this ontology [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

Śrīnivāsa states the school position with full symmetry of denial: the *jīva* is neither *atyantabhinna* nor *atyantābhinna* with respect to Śrī Puruṣottama, but *paramātmano 'ṃśaḥ* [[BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)]. That *aṃśa* is explicitly glossed as *śaktirūpa*, and the *jīva* is accordingly described as *alpaśakti* and *asvatantra* [[BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)]. The term therefore secures real difference and non-difference within an asymmetrical dependence-structure, rather than licensing mutual convertibility of *jīva* and Īśvara [[BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)].

Vallabha states without attenuation that the *jīva* is *brahmaṇo 'ṃśaḥ*, grounding the claim in *nānāvyapadeśa* and confirming it from Bhagavad-Gītā 15.7 [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. At the same time, whether Brahman is to be called *niraṃśa* or *sāṃśa* is not to be settled from ordinary experience at all, since Brahman is *vedaika-samadhigamya* [[BSB 2.3.43-45c](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45c)]. *Aṃśa* here names a real manifestation of Brahman disclosed by śruti and smṛti, not a merely figurative or subtractive partition [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)][[BSB 2.3.43-45c](cite://vallabha/anubhasya/2.3.43-45c)].

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'part.' In English that usually suggests a detachable piece cut from a larger whole. Most Sanskrit debates are precisely about refusing that picture while still preserving dependence, derivation, or manifestation. Translating *aṃśa* without noting whether the school means mode, power, transformation, or only figurative belonging imports a crude mereology the texts are arguing over.
